item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd been wanting to find and read this one again! Luckily in my going down the list I've found it! Just as good as it was first time I read it except I'm adding a +1 this time!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is a treat for everest nerds. I'm disabled, i could never even attempt to summit, but the mountaineer obsession w/Everest has long fascinated me. that drive to summit 'because it's there' (I squealed a little at that hat tip) despite the fact that you'll likely see corpses on the way up… i could go on. i wonder if 1529 has anything to do with that compulsion. +1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as no source found indicates it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Saw this comment and I figure for future readers of SCP-1529's comments it'd be good to have some information buried somewhere in here.

The corpse's name is Hannelore Schmatz, she died in 1979 which at this time of this comment is about 39 years ago. Her husband Gerhard Schmatz died in 2005, about 13 years ago. As far as I can find Hannelore had no children, and as far as living relatives go there are none. Nature also was a bit disrespectful and used wind to push her body off the mountain.

<<< Personal Opinion>>>
Personal Opinion

Personally, I enjoyed this SCP. When you take into account that some of the mainlisters include a unkillable lizard, a statue that murders you if you blink, and a disfigured monstrosity that will tear you to shreds if you look at its face. I feel like murderous mountaineer that freezes people to death on Mount Everest fits right in. Especially when there is just the right amount of mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know this is a really old article at this point but it bothers me that it uses a picture of a real person's corpse who died not too long ago and who most likely has living relatives. Everest's death zone is both morbidly fascinating and disturbing on its own and this article just seems to be piggybacking on those concepts without bringing much else to the table.
It has some good ideas in it but I can't bring myself to like it when it's not prepared to lets itself stand on its own merits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember reading a Reddit comment that said something along the lines of "SCP-096 evolved its characteristics to be able to fight against SCP-1529." Makes you think a little bit. This is a pretty good skip, and it works pretty well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this SCP. It's a humanoid monster that's creepy and mysterious without being too pretentious. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
my only problem is that you redacted what it did to them
other than that, it was pretty good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd like drop a little bit of info here, as I've been reading a bit about Everest over the past week.

The picture at the top of the article is of the body of Hannelore Schmatz, a German mountaineer who in 1979 became the first woman and the first German to die on Everest's upper slopes, while on an expedition with her husband Gerhard Schmatz, Swiss-American climber Ray Genet, and 5 others.

Hannelore and Genet had both summited on October 1st, but became exhausted on the way back down to camp. Against the urging of their Sherpa guides, they chose to bivouac when it became dark out. That night, a heavy storm bore down on the group. Genet was dead by the morning of October 2, while Hannelore survived the night and continued down. About 100 meters above the camp, she sat down and froze to death. Hannelore's guide, Sungdare Sherpa, survived, but lost most of his fingers.

Hannelore Schmatz's body remained on the South Col of Everest for long after she died, still visible to climbers, while Ray Genet's body vanished under the snow. In 1984, a Nepalese police inspector and his guide both fell to their deaths while attempting to recover her body. Hannelore's body was eventually blown down the east face of the mountain by high winds, where it remains to this day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It does "something" to people that drains the heat from them. AN MY HORRIBLE MIND CAN'T STOP IMAGINING IT HAS SOMETHING TO DO WITH ITS PENIS, WHAT HAVE YOU DONE TO ME?!

This scip is truly great, I enjoy human-like monsters the most, and this one is fascinating and crepy. Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love :D +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy shit. Is that his brain in the picture?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didnt really like the begining of this one, it seemed super overhyped for its rating. But the interview makes it impossible to downvote, which kind of explains it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The subject matter was an immediate turn off for me. Never been a fan of anything snow related. In the end, I really enjoyed this skip. The expungements got some extremely disturbing ideas flowing through my brain. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy shit… I’m going to cross ‘‘climb Mount Everest’’ off of the list of things I always wanted to do.
All hail the King in the North.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The second instance of SCP-1529 is on Gangkhar Puensum in Bhutan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh.

See, when George Mallory gave that answer, I thought he meant 'because that giant-ass mountain is there', not 'because the motivation is there.'

So should I actually interpret the answer as the ladder?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The line makes perfect sense. 1529's motivations for what it does are very similar to those of the people climbing the mountain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why not? Why not let him die a natural death (as natural as mesothelioma can get) and instead have Mountain King outstretch his powers all the way to Australia to kill him? That's some seriously bad sportsmanship on Mountain King's part.

Also, it should be 'no individual challenges the King of the Mountain.' I'd like to believe that Tenzing took a pickaxe to Mountain King's head when Hillary got in trouble.

And is it not possible to change that 'Because it's there' line? Because I still find it too cheesy, and it barely makes any sense in context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No happy endings, man. In the end, noone challenges the King of the Mountain in his domain and comes out on top.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I admit that this was a really good article, but I hate how you used Lincoln Hall (or a person based off of him).

Sure, it's awesome he managed to survive the encounter long enough to tell the tale, but for the SCP to kill him anyway seems like a kick in the balls.

Also, the use of the "Because it's there" line comes off as extremely cheesy to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I normally hate [DATA EXPUNGED]. I think this article should be one of the ones people point to and say "This, this right here? That's how you do it." Having no idea what's behind those tags fills me with dread instead of annoyance.

Even beyond that, overal great article. Deserves every upvote it has, including mine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent use of expungement. I have a direction without knowing exactly what is behind it and I'm okay with that.

+1 you monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really loved this, it put a real shiver up my spine. (Pun absolutely intended)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I were writing this now, instead of it having been my first SCP, I probably wouldn't have expunged so much. As it is, though, I think it reads better with the expungements than if I put in exactly what it does. IMO, a large part of what makes this article work is that there's so much about it that's unknowable, because venturing into its habitat is incredibly dangerous and has a good chance of killing you even if you don't encounter it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it would be a little better if you gave us HINTS as to what was behind the expunged. While many peoples' minds fill in the blanks with creepy, others are a little odd.
But that's your call. +1 anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even though this isn't nightmare-causing-ly horrifying, it's still incredibly creepy, and the amount of research that have went into this is truly awe-inspiring. I particularly like the hint that even though it rarely moves below the 8000 metre mark, it is definitely capable of going lower and has a rather terrifyingly long range. I agree with the criticism that the interview log was the tiniest bit melodramatic in a few places, but the rest of the article was so well-written that I still happily upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The bit about the goggleyes and the [DATA EXPUNGED] behind them cinched the article for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am absolutely elated to see that the first SCP I ever wrote has managed to break the coveted +100 mark (though it still took about two months longer than SCP-1981, grumble grumble). It was the kernel of this idea that got me back into writing for fun after years of not doing much of anything, and i'd again like to credit the late Lincoln Hall, whose death a week before I posted this provided the inspiration for the interview log. Thanks for the support!

(gets played off by the orchestra)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've always been morbidly fascinated with the fact that climbing Everest involves contact with so many corpses, and it's nice to see that you've really put the work in, the route is accurate and the reference to Green Boots is nice. Definite +1
Congratulations, Smapti!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cons: All in all, it's a thing up in the mountains that kills people. Too many expungements that leave too much to imagination.
Pros: Did the research. Made me do the research. A rather unusual setting. L. is inspiring.
Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love it. One of the first times I've gotten that tingly-spine feeling while reading, in a long while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, this is great. Was coping ok with it until I read the log which freaked me right out. +1 for you sir, +10 if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The number of people who have died near the summit of Everest is public knowledge, so I couldn't very well redact that. (It's actually closer to 220, but I rounded down.) I wanted to imply that the creature has been responsible for a significantly large number of those deaths and that a very small number of its victims have survived, thus the redacted numbers being 3 digits and 1 digit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I imagine that if the creature were ever captured and interviewed, it would call Boukreev a worthy foe and mourn his death.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who also loves reading about Everest, I just wanted to tell you I think this is brilliant!

If it were possible to add -J logs to normal SCP articles, I'd love to see hand-to-hand combat between it and Anatoli Boukreev.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To address a few points people have brought up;

There are more "appears" left in the article, but those have at least some reason to be there. Mixing them up with some synonyms wouldn't hurt, through.

You're right, I do appear to use "appears" a lot. Most of what the Foundation knows about the creature is inference and speculation - they're going by pictures taken from space, through telescopes miles away, and from a handful of eyewitness accounts from people who were confused and delirious. They can't specifically judge its height and weight, or whether its coverings are clothes or part of its body, or how long it takes its victims to die. I'll give the article a review and see if I can mix it up a bit.

The inteview is overly melodramatic in some places.

It's supposed to be. L. isn't a Foundation agent who's used to speaking clinically - he's an ordinary person who went through a near-fatal experience with a monster.

This is almost identical to a Warhammer monster, so although I like the execution I'll have to withold a vote for now.

THE EVEREST ELIMINATOR STRIKES AGAIN?

I wasn't actually aware of either of those references when I wrote this.

This is one of those entries that makes me REALLY, REALLY want to know what's happening behind the DE's. In a good way. And while individual head-canon is always at play, I kinda want to know if you had a particular thing in mind for what exactly this thing does to people.

I'm not 100% on how it works physically, but the creature is somehow interacting with the person and draining the heat out of its body, which leads to the accelerated death from hypothermia. It's messy, physically invasive, and painful - both for the victim and the creature. The creature isn't some sort of heat vampire - it's not "feeding" on the heat in any way, and in fact it probably makes it very uncomfortable since its body temperature is naturally very low. As per the interview log, its motives are pretty similar to those of the climbers themselves - it does it because it's difficult, challenging, and thrilling, "because it's there".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Appears to kill you horribly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very well written, I like it alot. Good job

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I'm just confused by numbers. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the most original and interesting SCPs I read lately. Solid upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1529 appears to be of average height and weight

but appears to show no correlation to weather

SCP-1529 appears to prefer to target solo climbers

Death from hypothermia appears to occur within 1-2 hours

The last one is particulary bad. The death appears to occur, but in reality THEY ARE STILL ALIVE! MUAHAHAHAHA! Anyway. There are more "appears" left in the article, but those have at least some reason to be there. Mixing them up with some synonyms wouldn't hurt, through.

The inteview is overly melodramatic in some places.

Don't really like it much. Just another thing that kills you horribly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I have an issue with some things (redacting the amount of victims from a total of 200 which is a 3 digit number anyways, as well as the number of survivors) , this is very well executed.
Upvote. This thing would make a lovely document drop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is outstanding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. The use of expungement is awesome :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: THE EVEREST ELIMINATOR STRIKES AGAIN?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent. Though not very I'm-gonna-lose-sleep-over-this-creepy to me, it is just so well written an interesting. Like morscata12 said: it's a long one, but everything was there for a reason and it was an interesting read from start to finish. All the voices in my head are unanimous: upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Took care of the "i" and double-space problems. And "acclimitized".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kinda think the note at the end of the containment procedures should be moved to the end of the article. While I suppose it's placement is realistic for a document of such a kind, I personally feel like it's a part of the hook that only really has impact after I've read about the only confirmed instance. This is still excellent, though, and I'll upvote regardless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The [DATA EXPUNGED] about what SCP-1529 actually does irritates me a little, but not enough to keep me from upvoting. I think the tone is the primary reason i like this - it could be really cheesy and awful, but it's creepy and unpleasant in a good way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In brief…

As humans are warm-blooded animals, it's normal for our body temperature to be significantly higher than the surrounding environment. Our metabolism produces heat to regulate body functions, and under normal conditions it's produced rapidly enough to compensate for heat lost to the atmosphere. When the environment is so cold that heat is wicked away faster than it's generated, or the process of heat generation slows, you have hypothermia.

A body temperature of 27 would be considered "severe" hypothermia, at which point mental confusion sets in, movements become strained and difficult, and metabolism starts to slow or stop entirely. The creature is doing… something to its victims that causes their temperature to drop rapidly independent of the environment.

I hope that helps you enjoy the story a little more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe I don't understand how hypothermia works. Or else I don't understand how numbers work, because that is very true. Carry on!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
24 is 75 Fahrenheit, so room temperature. A body temperature of 27 is about 80 Fahrenheit, well below a standard body temp of 37. It's significant in that, even though the agent is in a heated room, contact with the creature is enough to induce severe hypothermia.

I'll clean up the interview and make it collapsible later in the day once I'm not trying to work from my phone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was really really good. It was a long article, but the length was perfect - everything was there for a reason. Nicely done on the pacing, the creep factor kept growing as the article went on. I like the subtle detail that this SCP is responsible for over half the deaths on Everest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wanted to hate the expungements, but I can't. This is just so good. Two things:

In the first incident, it's said the agent's body was 27 C when he was in a room that was 24 C. Now, while I have no idea what the significance of those numbers is (27 is lower than body temperature?), it seems odd that his being warmer than the room would be odd. I would expect him to be colder than the room, due to the effects of this thing.

Also, there are a ton of "i'd" that need capitalized in the interview log. Also, seconding the collapsible for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very neat! Might I recommend putting the interview with "L" into a collapsable since it is so long?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is almost identical to a Warhammer monster, so although I like the execution I'll have to withold a vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One of the things which bugs me a bit is the addition of photos in Mallory's camera. The Foundation didn't become aware of the monster until 1970 and yet apparently they seized Mallory's camera and reported it lost. Are they in the habit of routinely absconding with peoples' possession and suppressing all mention of them, or were they already suspecting something?

Mallory's corpse wasn't discovered until 1999, well after the Foundation would have become aware of the creature's existence. I guess I assumed that was well-known, but I'll add a line to clarify that.
